edges.description: Enhanced SHP2 phosphatase activity promotes RAS activation and downstream ERK signaling.
PTPN11-driven Noonan syndrome can include mutation-associated hematologic abnormalities, including bleeding diathesis and JMML risk.
Enhanced RAS-GTP loading directly amplifies RAF-MEK-ERK cascade activation.
Hyperactive RAF1 directly phosphorylates MEK, amplifying ERK signaling.
RAF1 kinase hyperactivation is strongly associated with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy development.
Aberrant RIT1-mediated RAF recruitment amplifies downstream ERK signaling.
RIT1 mutations are strongly associated with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
Accumulated RAS proteins lead to increased basal MAPK signaling.
Diverse rare Noonan genotypes affecting RAS-MAPK components still converge on excess ERK pathway output.
Perturbed ERK signaling alters endocardial-mesenchymal transition during valve development.
Sustained ERK signaling promotes hypertrophic growth and fetal gene reprogramming.
Sustained RAS-MAPK signaling perturbs cortical neuronal lineage patterning and synaptic maturation programs in Noonan syndrome models.
RAS-MAPK pathway overactivation contributes to clinically severe lymphatic anomalies in Noonan syndrome.
ERK hyperactivation in growth plate cartilage impairs chondrocyte differentiation and endochondral bone growth.
Dysregulated embryonic cardiac development in Noonan syndrome produces septation defects and additional right-sided outflow lesions beyond pulmonary valve dysplasia.
Chronic dysregulation of the RAS-MAPK pathway in Noonan syndrome contributes to elevated risk of benign and malignant neoplasms.
Impaired endochondral bone growth reduces linear growth and contributes to short stature.
Perturbed cortical development and connectivity likely contributes to delayed neurodevelopmental trajectories.
Abnormal cortical-layer identity and synaptic organization can contribute to persistent cognitive impairment in a subset of patients.
Prenatal lymphatic dysfunction can manifest as increased nuchal fluid on fetal ultrasound.
Structural and flow abnormalities in lymphatic channels manifest clinically as peripheral or generalized lymphedema.
Central conducting lymphatic dysfunction can produce chylous pleural effusions.
Bleeding diathesis and coagulation abnormalities manifest clinically as easy bruising or excessive bleeding.
Myeloid dysregulation in susceptible genotypes can progress to JMML in a small subset of affected individuals.
Valve morphogenesis defects in the pulmonary outflow tract manifest as dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis.
Abnormal cardiac septation can manifest as secundum or other atrial septal defects.
Abnormal ventricular septation can produce clinically significant ventricular septal defects.
Aberrant development of the pulmonary outflow tract can narrow the branch pulmonary arteries.
This shared RASopathy cancer-predisposition branch manifests clinically as increased risk of diverse solid and hematologic neoplasms.
Persistent cardiomyocyte hypertrophic remodeling yields clinical hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

nodes.description: Excessive or abnormal bleeding can occur in Noonan syndrome, especially in individuals with coagulation-factor defects or platelet dysfunction.

evidence.snippet: Most affected individuals have characteristic facial features that evolve with age; a broad, webbed neck; increased bleeding tendency; and a high incidence of congenital heart disease, failure to thrive, short stature, feeding difficulties, sternal deformity, renal malformation, pubertal delay, cryptorchidism, developmental or behavioral problems, vision problems, hearing loss, and lymphedema.
HP:0001892 | Abnormal bleeding | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Electrocardiographic abnormalities are common in Noonan syndrome and may accompany structural heart disease or cardiomyopathy surveillance.

evidence.snippet: HP:0003115 | Abnormal EKG | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Coagulation abnormalities are part of the Noonan syndrome bleeding diathesis and can affect perioperative management.

evidence.snippet: Other findings can include broad or webbed neck, unusual chest shape with superior pectus carinatum and inferior pectus excavatum, cryptorchidism, varied coagulation defects, lymphatic dysplasias, and ocular abnormalities.
HP:0001928 | Abnormality of coagulation | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Additional established Noonan genes affecting small GTPases, RAF/MEK kinases, and RasGAP regulation converge on increased RAS-MAPK pathway throughput despite diverse molecular entry points.

evidence.snippet: DIAGNOSIS/TESTING: The diagnosis of Noonan is established in a proband with suggestive findings and a heterozygous pathogenic variant in BRAF, KRAS, MAP2K1, MRAS, NRAS, PTPN11, RAF1, RASA2, RIT1, RRAS2, SOS1, or SOS2 or either a heterozygous variant or biallelic pathogenic variants in LZTR1 identified by molecular genetic testing.

nodes.description: Cardiac rhythm abnormalities are part of the broader cardiovascular phenotype spectrum in Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0011675 | Arrhythmia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Atrial septal defects occur in 6-10% of individuals with Noonan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Atrial Septal Defect Surgical Closure Following Trametinib Utilization in Noonan Syndrome-Associated Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: An infant presented with a large secundum atrial septal defect complicating NS-HCM.
HP:0001631 | Atrial septal defect | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Tumor spectrum in children with Noonan syndrome and SOS1 or RAF1 mutations.
evidence.snippet: We performed SOS1, RAF1, BRAF, MEK1, and MEK2 mutation analysis in a cohort of 102 PTPN11- and KRAS-negative NS patients and found pathogenic SOS1 mutations in 10, RAF1 mutations in 4, and BRAF mutations in 2 patients.
BRAF | B-Raf proto-oncogene, serine/threonine kinase | hgnc:1097 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
BRAF | HGNC:1097 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Moderate

nodes.description: Peripheral or branch pulmonary artery stenosis is a recognized additional right-sided outflow lesion in Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Other structural defects include atrial and ventricular septal defects, branch pulmonary artery stenosis, and tetralogy of Fallot.

nodes.description: Coagulation defects including factor XI deficiency and platelet dysfunction, leading to easy bruising and prolonged bleeding after surgery or trauma.

evidence.snippet: Most affected individuals have characteristic facial features that evolve with age; a broad, webbed neck; increased bleeding tendency; and a high incidence of congenital heart disease, failure to thrive, short stature, feeding difficulties, sternal deformity, renal malformation, pubertal delay, cryptorchidism, developmental or behavioral problems, vision problems, hearing loss, and lymphedema.
HP:0000978 | Bruising susceptibility | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Balloon valvuloplasty or surgical valvotomy for pulmonary valve stenosis; septal myectomy or alcohol ablation for severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Atrial Septal Defect Surgical Closure Following Trametinib Utilization in Noonan Syndrome-Associated Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: The patient was treated with trametinib to improve cardiac hypertrophy and then underwent successful surgical closure of the atrial septal defect.

nodes.description: In endocardial and valvular tissues, perturbed ERK signaling alters endocardial-mesenchymal transition and valve morphogenesis, underlying pulmonary valve stenosis, the most common cardiac defect in Noonan syndrome.

biological_processes: epithelial to mesenchymal transition involved in endocardial cushion formation

evidence.reference_title: PTPN11 mutations in Noonan syndrome: molecular spectrum, genotype-phenotype correlation, and phenotypic heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: pulmonic stenosis was more prevalent among the group of subjects with NS who had PTPN11 mutations than it was in the group without them (70.6% vs. 46.2%; P<.01)

nodes.description: In cardiomyocytes, sustained ERK signaling (and intersecting AKT/mTOR activity) promotes hypertrophic growth and fetal gene reprogramming, leading to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, particularly in patients with RAF1 and RIT1 mutations.

evidence.reference_title: Gain-of-function RAF1 mutations cause Noonan and LEOPARD syndromes with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: Of 19 subjects with a RAF1 mutation in two hotspots, 18 (or 95%) showed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), compared with the 18% prevalence of HCM among individuals with Noonan syndrome in general.

evidence.reference_title: Gain-of-function mutations in RIT1 cause Noonan syndrome, a RAS/MAPK pathway syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Seventy percent of mutation-positive individuals presented with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; this frequency is high relative to the overall 20% incidence in individuals with Noonan syndrome.

nodes.description: Central conducting lymphatic dysfunction can manifest as fetal or postnatal chylous pleural effusions.

evidence.reference_title: Central conducting lymphatic anomaly: from bench to bedside.
evidence.snippet: Central conducting lymphatic anomaly (CCLA) is a complex lymphatic anomaly characterized by abnormalities of the central lymphatics and may present with nonimmune fetal hydrops, chylothorax, chylous ascites, or lymphedema.

nodes.description: Beyond pulmonary valve dysplasia, disturbed embryonic cardiac morphogenesis in Noonan syndrome can produce septation defects and branch pulmonary artery abnormalities.

evidence.snippet: Other structural defects include atrial and ventricular septal defects, branch pulmonary artery stenosis, and tetralogy of Fallot.

nodes.description: NS-derived cortical organoid models show abnormal excitatory-neuron layer specification and reduced synaptic connectivity, consistent with neurodevelopmental pathway disruption.

evidence.reference_title: Aberrant cortical layer development of brain organoids developed from Noonan syndrome-iPSCs
evidence.snippet: Surprisingly, EN subpopulation co-expressing upper layer marker SATB2 and deep layer maker CTIP2 was enriched in NS-COs during the cortical development.

nodes.description: Undescended testes in males is a common finding.

evidence.reference_title: PTPN11 mutations in Noonan syndrome: molecular spectrum, genotype-phenotype correlation, and phenotypic heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: The prevalence of other congenital heart malformations, short stature, pectus deformity, cryptorchidism, and developmental delay did not differ between the two groups.
HP:0000028 | Cryptorchidism | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Prenatal cystic hygroma can occur as part of the Noonan syndrome lymphatic dysplasia spectrum.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000476 | Cystic hygroma | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Delayed skeletal maturation can accompany postnatal growth impairment in Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002750 | Delayed skeletal maturation | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Downward slanting of the eye openings is a common facial feature.

evidence.snippet: The main facial features of NS are hypertelorism with down-slanting palpebral fissures, ptosis and low-set posteriorly rotated ears with a thickened helix.
HP:0000494 | Downslanted palpebral fissures | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Convergent point where all upstream RAS-MAPK pathway defects lead to sustained ERK1/2 phosphorylation and hyperactivation. This affects cell proliferation, differentiation, and survival during embryonic development and postnatal life.

biological_processes: regulation of ERK1 and ERK2 cascade

evidence.reference_title: Germline gain-of-function mutations in SOS1 cause Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: SHP2 is required for RAS-ERK MAP kinase (MAPK) cascade activation, and Noonan syndrome mutants enhance ERK activation ex vivo and in mice.

nodes.description: Developmental support including speech therapy, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and special education services for developmental delays and learning disabilities.

evidence.snippet: Developmental disabilities are addressed by early intervention programs and individualized education strategies.

nodes.description: Many infants experience feeding difficulties and failure to thrive in early life.

evidence.snippet: Other associated features are webbed neck, chest deformity, mild intellectual deficit, cryptorchidism, poor feeding in infancy, bleeding tendency and lymphatic dysplasias.
HP:0008872 | Feeding difficulties in infancy | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Mild cognitive impairment and motor delays are common, with learning disabilities in approximately 25% of individuals.

evidence.reference_title: PTPN11 mutations in Noonan syndrome: molecular spectrum, genotype-phenotype correlation, and phenotypic heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: The prevalence of other congenital heart malformations, short stature, pectus deformity, cryptorchidism, and developmental delay did not differ between the two groups.
HP:0012758 | Neurodevelopmental delay | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Recombinant human growth hormone (rhGH) is FDA-approved for treatment of short stature in Noonan syndrome. Can improve final adult height by approximately 1 standard deviation.

evidence.reference_title: Noonan syndrome spectrum disorders in real life: patient characteristics and response to growth hormone therapy in a genetically defined single-country multicenter cohort.
evidence.snippet: GH therapy was associated with an increase in height SDS from -2.92 to -1.97 (median) following 5 years, and to -1.68 in those with final height.

nodes.description: Local RAS-ERK hyperactivation in the growth plate impairs chondrocyte differentiation during endochondral bone growth, contributing to the characteristic postnatal growth impairment of Noonan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Noonan syndrome-causing SHP2 mutants impair ERK-dependent chondrocyte differentiation during endochondral bone growth.
evidence.snippet: Growth retardation is a constant feature of Noonan syndrome (NS) but its physiopathology remains poorly understood.

nodes.description: Conductive or sensorineural hearing problems occur in a subset of individuals and justify formal audiology surveillance.

evidence.snippet: Most affected individuals have characteristic facial features that evolve with age; a broad, webbed neck; increased bleeding tendency; and a high incidence of congenital heart disease, failure to thrive, short stature, feeding difficulties, sternal deformity, renal malformation, pubertal delay, cryptorchidism, developmental or behavioral problems, vision problems, hearing loss, and lymphedema.

nodes.description: PTPN11-associated Noonan syndrome can include mutation-associated bleeding diathesis and predisposition to juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia, indicating a distinct hematologic disease branch.

evidence.reference_title: Protein-tyrosine phosphatase, nonreceptor type 11 mutation analysis and clinical assessment in 45 patients with Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: hematological abnormalities, such as bleeding diathesis and juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia, were exclusively present in mutation-positive patients (5 of 18 vs. 0 of 27; P = 0.007).

nodes.description: A high-arched palate is part of the craniofacial phenotype spectrum of Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000218 | High palate | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Widely spaced eyes are a characteristic facial feature of Noonan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Novel characterization of MRAS mutation-associated Noonan syndrome: Mild adult-onset hypertrophic cardiomyopathy combined with infective endocarditis: A case report.
evidence.snippet: A 22-year-old woman presented with typical dysmorphic features of NS, including short stature, broad forehead, hypertelorism, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, and a broad neck.
HP:0000316 | Hypertelorism | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Left ventricular hypertrophy occurring in approximately 20% of patients overall, but up to 70-95% in those with RAF1 or RIT1 mutations. Can be present at birth or develop during infancy.

evidence.reference_title: Gain-of-function RAF1 mutations cause Noonan and LEOPARD syndromes with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: Of 19 subjects with a RAF1 mutation in two hotspots, 18 (or 95%) showed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), compared with the 18% prevalence of HCM among individuals with Noonan syndrome in general.

evidence.reference_title: Gain-of-function mutations in RIT1 cause Noonan syndrome, a RAS/MAPK pathway syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Seventy percent of mutation-positive individuals presented with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; this frequency is high relative to the overall 20% incidence in individuals with Noonan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: PTPN11 mutations in Noonan syndrome: molecular spectrum, genotype-phenotype correlation, and phenotypic heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: hypertrophic cardiomyopathy was less prevalent among those with PTPN11 mutations (5.9% vs. 26.2%; P<.005)
HP:0001639 | Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Pubertal and gonadal-axis abnormalities can occur in Noonan syndrome, including hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000044 | Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Decreased muscle tone is a frequent neurologic or neuromotor manifestation in Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001252 | Hypotonia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Increased nuchal fluid or translucency on prenatal ultrasound can be an early clue to Noonan syndrome, especially when the fetal karyotype is normal.

evidence.snippet: NS should be considered in all foetuses with polyhydramnion, pleural effusions, oedema and increased nuchal fluid with a normal karyotype.

nodes.description: Increased joint mobility is a common musculoskeletal feature of Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001382 | Joint hypermobility | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: A subset of individuals with Noonan syndrome, especially with specific PTPN11 variants, can develop juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia as part of the syndrome's hematologic cancer predisposition spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: Protein-tyrosine phosphatase, nonreceptor type 11 mutation analysis and clinical assessment in 45 patients with Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: hematological abnormalities, such as bleeding diathesis and juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia, were exclusively present in mutation-positive patients (5 of 18 vs. 0 of 27; P = 0.007).

evidence.reference_title: Genotypic and phenotypic characterization of Noonan syndrome: new data and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: In one patient with NS and mild juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (JMML) the mutation 218C --> T (Thr73Ile) was found. This confirms previous findings indicating that individuals with NS with specific mutations in PTPN11 are at risk of developing JMML.

evidence.reference_title: Germline gain-of-function mutations in SOS1 cause Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: KRAS mutations account for <5% of cases of Noonan syndrome
KRAS | KRAS proto-oncogene, GTPase | hgnc:6407 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
KRAS | HGNC:6407 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Definitive

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-Phenotype Analysis and New Clinical Findings in a Series of 24 Patients Presenting with Noonan Syndrome and Related Disorders.
evidence.snippet: Final diagnoses included 15 individuals with Noonan syndrome...one each in LZTR1, A2ML1, and MRAS...
LZTR1 | leucine zipper like post translational regulator 1 | hgnc:6742 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
LZTR1 | HGNC:6742 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Definitive
LZTR1 | HGNC:6742 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Loss of LZTR1-mediated RAS proteostasis through CRL3 E3 ligase increases RAS-family protein levels (including MRAS, RIT1, and KRAS) and MAPK signaling. Dominant LZTR1 mutations act in a dominant-negative manner to disrupt ubiquitination and degradation of RAS proteins.

evidence.reference_title: Dysregulation of RAS proteostasis by autosomal-dominant LZTR1 mutation induces Noonan syndrome-like phenotypes in mice.
evidence.snippet: Cardiomyocyte size and the expression of RAS subfamily members, including MRAS and RIT1, were significantly increased in the left ventricles (LVs) of mutant male mice.

nodes.description: Posteriorly rotated, low-set ears are characteristic.

evidence.reference_title: Novel characterization of MRAS mutation-associated Noonan syndrome: Mild adult-onset hypertrophic cardiomyopathy combined with infective endocarditis: A case report.
evidence.snippet: A 22-year-old woman presented with typical dysmorphic features of NS, including short stature, broad forehead, hypertelorism, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, and a broad neck.

nodes.description: Noonan syndrome can include severe central and peripheral lymphatic abnormalities that produce clinically significant fluid and lymphatic-flow complications.

evidence.snippet: Albeit phenotypically heterogeneous, NS can be associated with severe cardiovascular and lymphatic anomalies, potentially lethal during infancy, neonatal and fetal periods.

nodes.description: Peripheral lymphedema and central conducting lymphatic anomalies occur in a substantial minority. ERK/SOX18 signaling axis is implicated in lymphatic dysplasia.

evidence.snippet: Most affected individuals have characteristic facial features that evolve with age; a broad, webbed neck; increased bleeding tendency; and a high incidence of congenital heart disease, failure to thrive, short stature, feeding difficulties, sternal deformity, renal malformation, pubertal delay, cryptorchidism, developmental or behavioral problems, vision problems, hearing loss, and lymphedema.
HP:0001004 | Lymphedema | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Next-generation sequencing identifies rare variants associated with Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We identified gain-of-function alleles in Ras-like without CAAX 1 (RIT1) and mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase 1 (MAP2K1) and previously unseen loss-of-function variants in RAS p21 protein activator 2 (RASA2) that are likely to cause NS in these patients.
MAP2K1 | HGNC:6840 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Limited

nodes.description: Trametinib (MEK1/2 inhibitor) has shown promising results in case reports for treatment-refractory hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and lymphatic complications. MEK inhibition can reverse cardiomyocyte hypertrophy in animal models.

evidence.reference_title: Atrial Septal Defect Surgical Closure Following Trametinib Utilization in Noonan Syndrome-Associated Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: Trametinib, an MEK inhibitor that attenuates abnormal signaling in the RAS/MAPK pathway, has been shown to improve NS-HCM outcomes.

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-Phenotype Analysis and New Clinical Findings in a Series of 24 Patients Presenting with Noonan Syndrome and Related Disorders.
evidence.snippet: Final diagnoses included 15 individuals with Noonan syndrome...one each in LZTR1, A2ML1, and MRAS...

evidence.reference_title: Novel characterization of MRAS mutation-associated Noonan syndrome: Mild adult-onset hypertrophic cardiomyopathy combined with infective endocarditis: A case report.
evidence.snippet: Whole-exome sequencing identified a heterozygous MRAS c.203C>T (p.Thr68Ile) mutation affecting a highly conserved residue among RASopathy-associated GTPases, supporting the diagnosis of MRAS-associated Noonan syndrome complicated by infective endocarditis.
MRAS | muscle RAS oncogene homolog | hgnc:7227 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
MRAS | HGNC:7227 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Moderate

nodes.description: Approximately 15-35% of individuals have mild intellectual disability.

evidence.snippet: Up to one fourth of affected individuals have mild intellectual disability, and language impairments in general are more common in NS than in the general population.
HP:0001249 | Intellectual disability | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: A restricted spectrum of NRAS mutations causes Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Here we report that germline NRAS mutations conferring enhanced stimulus-dependent MAPK activation account for some cases of this disorder.
NRAS | NRAS proto-oncogene, GTPase | hgnc:7989 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
NRAS | HGNC:7989 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Definitive

evidence.reference_title: PTPN11 mutations in Noonan syndrome: molecular spectrum, genotype-phenotype correlation, and phenotypic heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: Mutations were found in 54 of 119 (45%) unrelated individuals with sporadic or familial NS.
PTPN11 | protein tyrosine phosphatase non-receptor type 11 | hgnc:9644 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

nodes.description: Superior pectus carinatum can occur with inferior pectus excavatum as part of the characteristic Noonan syndrome chest-wall configuration.

evidence.snippet: Other findings can include broad or webbed neck, unusual chest shape with superior pectus carinatum and inferior pectus excavatum,
HP:0000768 | Pectus carinatum | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Chest wall deformities including pectus excavatum and pectus carinatum are common.

evidence.reference_title: PTPN11 mutations in Noonan syndrome: molecular spectrum, genotype-phenotype correlation, and phenotypic heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: The prevalence of other congenital heart malformations, short stature, pectus deformity, cryptorchidism, and developmental delay did not differ between the two groups.
HP:0000767 | Pectus excavatum | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Adult Noonan syndrome and Noonan syndrome with multiple lentigines can include neuropathic pain and objective peripheral nerve involvement, with enlarged nerves and impaired quality of life in symptomatic individuals.

evidence.reference_title: Neuropathic Pain and Enlarged Nerves in Adult Noonan Syndrome and Noonan Syndrome With Multiple Lentigines: Health-Related Quality of Life and Neurologic Symptoms.
evidence.snippet: All patients reported somatosensory symptoms consistent with peripheral neuropathy...electrodiagnostic testing was consistent with peroneal neuropathy in one patient.

nodes.description: Physical therapy to address motor delays and hypotonia common in Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Physiotherapy and/or speech therapy should be offered if indicated.

nodes.description: Posterior rotation of the ears is a characteristic ear-position finding in Noonan syndrome and is often described together with low-set ears.

evidence.reference_title: Novel characterization of MRAS mutation-associated Noonan syndrome: Mild adult-onset hypertrophic cardiomyopathy combined with infective endocarditis: A case report.
evidence.snippet: A 22-year-old woman presented with typical dysmorphic features of NS, including short stature, broad forehead, hypertelorism, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, and a broad neck.
HP:0000358 | Posteriorly rotated ears | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Drooping of the upper eyelids is frequently observed.

evidence.snippet: The main facial features of NS are hypertelorism with down-slanting palpebral fissures, ptosis and low-set posteriorly rotated ears with a thickened helix.
HP:0000508 | Ptosis | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis is the most common cardiac defect, occurring in approximately 50-60% of patients. Associated with PTPN11 and SOS1 genotypes.

evidence.reference_title: PTPN11 mutations in Noonan syndrome: molecular spectrum, genotype-phenotype correlation, and phenotypic heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: pulmonic stenosis was more prevalent among the group of subjects with NS who had PTPN11 mutations than it was in the group without them (70.6% vs. 46.2%; P<.01)
HP:0001641 | Abnormal pulmonary valve morphology | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Gain-of-function RAF1 mutations cause Noonan and LEOPARD syndromes with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: 18 of 231 individuals with Noonan syndrome without known mutations (corresponding to 3% of all affected individuals)...have missense mutations in RAF1
RAF1 | Raf-1 proto-oncogene, serine/threonine kinase | hgnc:9829 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) (gain of function) in
RAF1 | HGNC:9829 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: RAF1 mutations, particularly those altering Ser259 and flanking residues, disrupt 14-3-3 binding and autoinhibition, resulting in constitutively elevated serine-threonine kinase activity and enhanced MEK phosphorylation.

evidence.reference_title: Gain-of-function RAF1 mutations cause Noonan and LEOPARD syndromes with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: Most mutations altered a motif flanking Ser259, a residue critical for autoinhibition of RAF1 through 14-3-3 binding.

evidence.reference_title: Next-generation sequencing identifies rare variants associated with Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We identified gain-of-function alleles in Ras-like without CAAX 1 (RIT1) and mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase 1 (MAP2K1) and previously unseen loss-of-function variants in RAS p21 protein activator 2 (RASA2) that are likely to cause NS in these patients.
RASA2 | RAS p21 protein activator 2 | hgnc:9872 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) (loss of function) in
RASA2 | HGNC:9872 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Limited

nodes.description: Noonan syndrome shares the broader RASopathy tendency toward benign and malignant neoplasia, reflecting chronic developmental dysregulation of the RAS-MAPK pathway.

evidence.snippet: When compared with the general population, children with RASopathies are at significantly increased risk of benign and malignant neoplasms.

evidence.reference_title: Gain-of-function mutations in RIT1 cause Noonan syndrome, a RAS/MAPK pathway syndrome.
evidence.snippet: we identified a total of nine missense, nonsynonymous mutations in RIT1...in 17 of 180 individuals (9%) with Noonan syndrome
RIT1 | Ras like without CAAX 1 | hgnc:10023 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) (gain of function) in
RIT1 | HGNC:10023 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: RIT1 gain-of-function mutations cause aberrant membrane localization and RAF recruitment, bypassing normal RAS regulation and driving excessive MAPK pathway activation.

evidence.reference_title: Gain-of-function mutations in RIT1 cause Noonan syndrome, a RAS/MAPK pathway syndrome.
evidence.snippet: These results demonstrate that gain-of-function mutations in RIT1 cause Noonan syndrome and show a similar biological effect to mutations in other RASopathy-related genes.

evidence.reference_title: Activating Mutations of RRAS2 Are a Rare Cause of Noonan Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: By using a mixed strategy of functional candidacy and exome sequencing, we identify RRAS2 as a gene implicated in NS in six unrelated subjects/families.
RRAS2 | RAS related 2 | hgnc:17271 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
RRAS2 | HGNC:17271 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: PTPN11 mutations destabilize the autoinhibitory interaction between the N-SH2 and PTP domains, resulting in constitutively elevated phosphatase activity. SHP2 is a positive regulator of RAS-MAPK signaling and gain-of-function mutations lead to enhanced ERK activation.

evidence.reference_title: PTPN11 mutations in Noonan syndrome: molecular spectrum, genotype-phenotype correlation, and phenotypic heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: All defects were missense, and several were recurrent. The vast majority of mutations altered amino acid residues located in or around the interacting surfaces of the N-SH2 and PTP domains

nodes.description: SOS-family gain-of-function mutations encode guanine nucleotide exchange factor variants with enhanced activity, increasing the rate of RAS-GDP to RAS-GTP conversion and amplifying downstream MAPK signaling.

evidence.reference_title: Germline gain-of-function mutations in SOS1 cause Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Noonan syndrome-associated SOS1 mutations are hypermorphs encoding products that enhance RAS and ERK activation.

evidence.reference_title: Germline gain-of-function mutations in SOS1 cause Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We identified missense mutations in SOS1, which encodes an essential RAS guanine nucleotide-exchange factor (RAS-GEF), in approximately 20% of cases of Noonan syndrome without PTPN11 mutation.
SOS1 | SOS Ras/Rac guanine nucleotide exchange factor 1 | hgnc:11187 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) (gain of function) in
SOS1 | HGNC:11187 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Definitive

evidence.reference_title: Rare variants in SOS2 and LZTR1 are associated with Noonan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We identified rare, segregating or de novo missense variants in SOS2 and LZTR1 in 4% and 8%, respectively, of the 50 Brazilian probands.
SOS2 | SOS Ras/Rho guanine nucleotide exchange factor 2 | hgnc:11188 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
SOS2 | HGNC:11188 | Noonan syndrome | MONDO:0018997 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: Spinal curvature is a recurrent musculoskeletal manifestation in Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002650 | Scoliosis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Postnatal growth retardation resulting in adult height typically at or below the third percentile for the general population. Linked to RAS/MAPK effects on GH signaling and chondrocyte differentiation.

evidence.reference_title: Noonan syndrome spectrum disorders in real life: patient characteristics and response to growth hormone therapy in a genetically defined single-country multicenter cohort.
evidence.snippet: Short stature is a key NSSD feature.
HP:0004322 | Short stature | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Speech and language therapy for articulation difficulties and language delays.

evidence.snippet: Physiotherapy and/or speech therapy should be offered if indicated.

nodes.description: Ocular misalignment is a frequent ophthalmologic manifestation requiring vision surveillance in Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000486 | Strabismus | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Thickened nuchal tissue or excess posterior neck skin is a frequent prenatal and early-life manifestation of Noonan syndrome lymphatic dysplasia.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000474 | Thickened nuchal skin fold | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: A triangular facial shape is a common component of the evolving Noonan syndrome facial gestalt.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000325 | Triangular face | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Noonan syndrome confers increased susceptibility to both hematologic and solid neoplasms, with reported tumors including rhabdomyosarcoma, Sertoli cell tumor, granular cell tumors, and giant cell tumor-like lesions in specific genotypic subsets.

evidence.reference_title: Tumor spectrum in children with Noonan syndrome and SOS1 or RAF1 mutations.
evidence.snippet: Three patients with SOS1 mutations presented with tumors (embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma, Sertoli cell testis tumor, and granular cell tumors of the skin). One patient with a RAF1 mutation had a lesion suggestive for a giant cell tumor.

nodes.description: Ventricular septal defects are part of the broader congenital heart disease spectrum in Noonan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Other structural defects include atrial and ventricular septal defects, branch pulmonary artery stenosis, and tetralogy of Fallot.

nodes.description: Excess skin on the lateral neck creating a webbed appearance.

evidence.snippet: Other associated features are webbed neck, chest deformity, mild intellectual deficit, cryptorchidism, poor feeding in infancy, bleeding tendency and lymphatic dysplasias.
HP:0000465 | Webbed neck | Very frequent (99-80%)
